title: Odds and Log-Odds
concept: Odds compare the probability of an event happening to it not happening, and log-odds make this relationship linear.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main benefit of using a Sigmoid function instead of a straight line for classification?

options.text: It guarantees the predicted probability stays between 0 and 1.
It makes the training process much faster.
It allows the model to predict multiple categories at once.

exercises.explain: Correct! A straight line can predict invalid probabilities like 150%, but the Sigmoid function keeps outputs strictly between 0 and 1.

cards.title: Betting on a coin toss
cards.body: If a coin is biased and lands on heads 80% of the time, we say the probability is 0.8. But a gambler might say the odds are 4 to 1 (80% chance of heads vs 20% chance of tails). Odds compare wins directly to losses.

cards.title: Odds and Log-Odds
cards.body: Odds is the ratio of probability p to 1-p. The Log-Odds (or logit) is the natural logarithm of the odds. It maps probability from [0, 1] to (-infinity, +infinity).

cards.title: The infinite accordion
cards.body: Imagine a 1-meter accordion. Sticking to probability restricts you to this small space. By converting to log-odds, you stretch the accordion out to stretch infinitely in both directions.

cards.title: Working the math
cards.body: If a team has a 75% chance (p=0.75) of winning, the odds are 0.75 / 0.25 = 3 (3 to 1). Taking the natural log of 3 gives a log-odds of 1.1. A 50% chance (p=0.5) has odds of 1 and a log-odds of 0.

cards.title: Why the extra math?
cards.body: A common myth is that log-odds are just useless math homework. In fact, they are the secret key: because log-odds range from negative to positive infinity, they allow us to use a simple straight line (w_0 + w_1 * x) to represent probabilities.

cards.title: Probability, Odds, and Log-Odds
cards.body: Comparing the three formats for representing likelihood:

left.text:
Probability: 0 to 1.
Odds: 0 to infinity.
Example: 80% prob = 4 to 1 odds.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If an event has a probability of 0.5, its odds are exactly 1.
exercises.explain: True! The odds are 0.5 / (1 - 0.5) = 1 (even odds of happening vs not happening).

exercises.prompt: Why does logistic regression predict log-odds instead of predicting probabilities directly using a linear formula?

options.text: Because log-odds can range from negative to positive infinity, matching a linear equation's potential output.
Because log-odds do not require computers to calculate.

exercises.explain: Perfect! A linear formula can output any real number. Mapping probabilities to log-odds matches this infinite range perfectly.

exercises.sentence: The mathematical ratio of the probability of an event occurring to the probability of it not occurring is called the ___.
exercises.explain: Spot on! The ratio p / (1-p) is the definition of odds.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following values represents a negative log-odds?
exercises.explain: Correct! When probability is less than 0.5, odds are less than 1, making their logarithm negative.

exercises.prompt: You are setting up a spam filter. If a message has a 50% probability of being spam, what are the odds and log-odds of it being spam?
exercises.explain: Incredible! When the probability is exactly 0.5, the odds are 0.5 / 0.5 = 1, and ln(1) = 0. Congratulations on mastering odds and log-odds! You've unlocked the math that connects lines to probabilities!
